title: Sensitive-data reveal vs password input vs input mask vs typed confirmation vs permission request
summary: Use sensitive-data reveal when a normally masked stored, generated, or displayed sensitive value needs controlled temporary exposure; use password input for entering an authentication secret, input mask for formatting structured entry, typed confirmation for visible target verification before a risky action, and permission request for OS or browser device-resource access.
decisionRules: Choose sensitive-data reveal when the value already exists in the product or is being reviewed and users need masked state, reveal action, hide action, hold-to-reveal, timed reveal, reauthentication, partial reveal, full reveal, copy while hidden, clipboard warning, audit event, redaction boundary, shoulder-surfing warning, expired reveal, screen reader announcement, or mobile compact treatment.

Choose password input when the user is entering a memorized secret into sign-in, login, password reset, or password creation; password reveal helps entry accuracy but the pattern is still the authentication input itself.

Choose input mask when the value has a stable visible format such as grouped digits, serial key, date, time, or account field and masking is about structured entry formatting rather than protecting a secret from observation.

Choose typed confirmation when users must type a visible target phrase such as a repository name to confirm a destructive action; masking that phrase prevents target verification and confuses it with a secret.

Choose permission request when the user is granting OS or browser access to a device resource such as camera, microphone, location, photos, contacts, notifications, Bluetooth, clipboard, or motion sensors rather than revealing a value the product already holds.
Use partial reveal when last-four, prefix plus suffix, issuer, domain, or token fingerprint is enough; use full reveal only when policy, role, and task need justify complete exposure.

Require reauthentication, role check, reason capture, audit event, or approval before revealing high-risk credentials, recovery codes, API keys, financial identifiers, health identifiers, or customer personal data.
A reveal control must have an immediate hide path, and visible values should auto-hide on timeout, blur, navigation, or expired reveal where policy requires it.
Copy while hidden must still warn about clipboard exposure and confirm copy without showing the raw value in status text, logs, URLs, analytics, screenshots, or support payloads.
A redaction boundary means the full value must not be sent to the client; CSS masking or DOM-only hiding is not enough for users who are not permitted to reveal the value.
failureModes: The secret is visible by default, permanently visible after reveal, or revealed automatically on hover, focus, validation, print, export, or blur.
The reveal button sits beside an unrelated action and causes accidental disclosure.
Copy places the raw secret on the clipboard silently while the interface appears masked.
A partial-view user receives the full raw value in the DOM and the UI hides it with styling only.
An audit record, console log, URL, analytics event, error message, screenshot, or support payload contains the raw sensitive value.
The UI offers Show but no Hide, no timeout, no shoulder-surfing warning, no reauthentication, or no audit event for high-risk values.

sources.note: Supports display option for entered passwords, paste support, password managers, and observation-risk framing.
Supports masked default, revealed password state, focus loss safeguards, script-change safeguards, and native reveal behavior.
Supports password box reveal, peek mode, spacing to avoid accidental reveal, and focus-dependent reveal availability.
Supports non-password masked inputs for secret keys, tokens, certificates, copy behavior, and visual-obfuscation limits.
Supports testing for sensitive information disclosure through UI components.
